Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Alif, Lam, Meem. Do the people think that they will be left to say, "We believe" and they will not be tried? (1-2)

Alif, Lam, Meem is the name of this Surah. Its detailed explanation has passed in Surah al-Baqarah.

Regarding 'Do the people think that...' (Ahasiban-nasu an...), although the statement is phrased in general terms, the reference is to those who had grown weary of the trial-filled life in Makkah. Some people had entered Islam, but they had no estimate of the hardships of this path. They assumed that once they stepped onto the path of virtue, they would find it smooth and reach their destination easily like travelling on a comfortable road. When these people faced heart-rending trials at the hands of the disbelievers, their footsteps faltered, and they began to harbor the kind of doubts that typically plague weak-minded people under such circumstances. For example: 'If this is the path of Allah, why is it so difficult? If the one inviting to it is indeed the Messenger of Allah, why is believing in him such a hazardous task? When we have stood up for the cause of Allah, we are serving the faith; why then have these hurdles and obstacles been placed in our path?' The rise of such questions is proof that these people were not well-acquainted with the path of truth and its demands, and could easily fall into the trial of hypocrisy. In this Surah, their spiritual ailment is addressed first. It is stated that if people assumed that they would merely claim faith and, on the basis of this claim alone, their names would be registered as sincere believers without their truthfulness being tested, then they had understood it completely wrong.

And We have already tried those before them, and Allah will surely make evident those who are truthful, and He will surely make evident the liars. (3)

In highly emphatic terms, Allah has reminded them of His established law (Sunnah) which inevitably confronts those who stand up with the claim of Islam and faith. He states that We tried those who stood up with this claim before them, and We will surely try them in the same manner. This refers to the past messengers and those who believed in them. 'And Allah will surely make evident those who are truthful, and He will surely make evident the liars' (falaya'lamannallahu-lladheena sadaqoo...) means: if this test does not occur, how will it be proven who is truthful in his claim and who is a liar? Therefore, Allah will surely distinguish between the upright and the false claimants. 'Knowledge' ('ilm) here is used in the sense of distinguishing and making evident, as explained in its respective place, and the verb is prefixed with particles of high emphasis. The meaning is that this is a fixed law of Allah; therefore, whether one finds it pleasing or unpleasing, everyone must pass through this test.

As far as Allah's knowledge is concerned, it encompasses everything. He is fully aware of what lies in the hearts of everyone—who is sincere and who is a hypocrite. However, He does not deal out reward and punishment to people solely on the basis of His own knowledge, but on the basis of what physically manifests from them. For this reason, He tests everyone through trials to evaluate them, and it is through these tests that the ranks of everyone are determined.

Or do those who reflect evil deeds think they will outrun Us? Evil is what they judge. (4)

After warning those who claim faith, the text warns those who were making the weak Muslims the target of their tyranny and oppression. It states that if these people assume they will be left to continue their oppression and will never be seized by Us, then their assumption is completely wrong. Although 'those who reflect evil deeds' (alladheena ya'maloonas-sayyi'at) is general in wording, the reference is to those wicked people who were inflicting tyranny on the helpless Muslims and, encouraged by God's respite, were growing bolder day by day. It states that if these people are under the illusion that God will continue to grant them respite forever, they are in a severe illusion.

'Evil is what they judge' (sa'a ma yahkumoon) means their judgment regarding God is extremely bad. If they have assumed Him to be weak, thinking He cannot seize them, their judgment is evil; and if they have assumed Him to be pleased with their tyranny or completely indifferent to it, their judgment is equally false and evil.

Whoever hopes for the meeting with Allah - then indeed, the term of Allah is coming. And He is the Hearing, the Knowing. (5)

This comforts the oppressed Muslims: remember that you must perform every deed in hope of the hereafter, and if you harbor this hope, rest assured that the term Allah has appointed for it will surely come to pass. A Day will come when you will meet your Lord, and on that Day, you will be fully rewarded for every hardship and pain you bore for His sake. God is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing. Nothing is outside the embrace of His knowledge; your sacrifices are in His knowledge, and the tyrannies of your enemies are also before Him.

And whoever strives only strives for himself. Indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds. (6)

This warns that anyone who believes and bears hardships in this path should remember that he is only securing his own future and benefit; he does not do any favor to God or His religion. Therefore, he should not feel disheartened when he faces a calamity. If he faces a calamity, God is not in need of anyone; He is free from need of the entire universe. Rather, humans are in need of Him; therefore, any sacrifice a person makes in the path of God is entirely for his own benefit, and brings no benefit to God. It should be noted that, like the world, religion is not something that serves God's needs, but serves the needs of His servants. Allah has appointed this path solely for human success. Just as a farmer works hard in his field for his own benefit, not for others, a human being travels the path of religion to reach his own destination, not God's.

And those who believe and do righteous deeds - We will surely remove from them their misdeeds and will surely reward them the best of what they used to do. (7)

Context indicates that 'misdeeds' (sayyi'at) here refers to minor sins and lapses. It states that Allah will deal with every believer who does righteous deeds by overlooking his lapses and rewarding his good deeds far beyond what they deserved. The meaning is that since God's deal with His servants is such that everyone will receive the reward for his every deed, why should any doubt arise in a person's heart that his suffering in His path was a favor to Allah or His religion?

And We have enjoined upon man goodness to his parents; but if they strive to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them. To Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do. (8)

In the trials mentioned in the previous verses, a major portion was the trial faced by the youth who accepted Islam at the hands of their disbelieving parents and guardians. Since the rights of parents are recognized in every religion, the disbelieving parents misused this right, trying to stop their children from fulfilling God's rights in the name of filial duty, and did not hesitate to inflict tyranny upon them. Examples of this exist in the history of every call to truth. The trials Abraham (peace be upon him) faced at the hands of his father have been detailed. The trials the youth faced during the mission of Moses (peace be upon him) have also been alluded to in previous Surahs. The same situation confronted the youth who believed in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), who became the target of tyranny at the hands of their parents for the crime of accepting Islam. This situation required that clear guidance be given to the youth regarding how to behave if their parents interfered in their faith. It is stated: 'We have enjoined upon man goodness to his parents, but if they strive to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them.' 'Enjoined' (wassayna) here is used in the sense of directing, and is used as such at various places in the Qur'an. The style indicates that the Qur'an has presented this as the established teaching of all previous religions and prophets. Indeed, the Torah, the Gospels, and the Psalms all place obedience to parents subordinate to obedience to God. The right parents possess over their children is granted to them by God; therefore, they have no right to stop their children from fulfilling the rights of God. When parents—whose right is the greatest after the right of God—do not possess this right, the question of others possessing it does not arise. Thus, the law in Islam is: 'There is no obedience to a creature in disobedience to the Creator.'

'That of which you have no knowledge' (ma laysa laka bihi 'ilm) is an argument refuting polytheism, presented in various styles throughout the Qur'an. 'Knowledge' ('ilm) here means proof or argument. The existence of one God is an obvious reality acknowledged by even the polytheists. As for His partners, producing proof is the responsibility of those who claim their partnership; unless there is proof, it is not permissible for any rational person to associate them with God and put the collar of their slavery around his neck.

'To Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do' contains both consolation and warning. It means: everyone's return is to Me, and none other can become a refuge; I will inform everyone of their deeds, meaning I will reward or punish them. I will call to account those who are inflicting tyranny on My servants today to stop them from My path, and I will reward My servants who show steadfastness in My path.

And those who believe and do righteous deeds - We will surely admit them among the righteous. (9)

While the previous verses highlighted warnings, this verse explicitly mentions consolation for the believers. 'Those who believe and do righteous deeds' (Alladheena aamanoo wa 'amilus-salihat) is used in its true sense here, meaning those who remain firm and steadfast in faith and righteous deeds despite the intense opposition of their enemies. It states that We will surely admit them among the righteous. 'The righteous' (as-saliheen) refers to the group of sincere servants of Allah who, having passed through the tests of this world, are chosen by Allah for the eternal kingdom of the hereafter. This group has no room for mere claimants; only those who prove their truthfulness on the touchstone of trials will find a place in it.

And of the people are some who say, "We believe in Allah," but when they are harmed for the cause of Allah, they consider the trial of the people as the punishment of Allah. And if victory comes from your Lord, they say, "Indeed, we were with you." Is not Allah most knowing of what is within the breasts of the worlds? (10)

This mentions those claimants of faith who made the claim but, when faced with trials, panicked at the harm inflicted by people as if they were facing the punishment of God. However, any suffering in this world is temporary, whereas the punishment of God is eternal. Therefore, bearing the greatest suffering in this world for the sake of the truth is far easier than inviting eternal punishment by yielding to the fear of people. In the law of trials on earth, everyone must choose between two paths: either choose the path of truth and face the opposition and tyranny of people with complete courage, or align with falsehood and face eternal loss in the hereafter. There is no other way.

It is noteworthy that the suffering inflicted by people is described as a 'trial' (fitnah) because its nature is temporary, whereas the punishment of Allah is described as 'punishment' (adhab) because it is absolute and inescapable.

'And if victory comes from your Lord, they say, "Indeed, we were with you"' means while they falter during trials and align with the enemies, they will claim a share in the success when the signs of victory appear, asserting that they made great sacrifices for the cause of truth.

'Is not Allah most knowing of what is within the breasts of the worlds?' (A-wa-laysallahu bi-a'lama bi-ma fee sudooril-alameen) means such claims can deceive only those who are ignorant of the secrets of the hearts; they cannot deceive the One to whom all secrets are manifest.

And Allah will surely make evident those who believe, and He will surely make evident the hypocrites. (11)

This means that since the hypocrites are mixed with the sincere believers, Allah will not leave them as they are; rather, He will distinguish and separate them. 'Those who believe' (alladheena aamanoo) refers to the sincere believers, contrasted with the hypocrites whose character was described in the previous verse—claiming faith but refusing to bear any hardship for it. This is stated in a highly emphatic manner, showing that making this distinction is a necessary requirement of His attributes; if He were not to do so, it would be contrary to His justice.

And those who disbelieve say to those who believe, "Follow our way, and we will carry your sins." And they will not carry anything of their sins. Indeed, they are liars. And they will surely carry their own burdens and [other] burdens with their own burdens, and they will surely be questioned on the Day of Resurrection about what they used to invent. (12-13)

Although 'those who disbelieve' (alladheena kafaroo) is general in wording, context indicates it refers to those disbelievers whose children or dependents had accepted Islam. These people would use coercion and moral pressure to turn their dependents away from Islam, saying: 'We understand good and evil better than you, so follow our way; if our way is wrong, its sin and consequence will be on our shoulders, and you will be free from responsibility, as we will carry your burden.' This logic has always been used by elders, guardians, parents, teachers, and leaders to influence their juniors, and it is often highly effective. To a certain extent, its influence is natural and harmless, but the matter of religion is of ultimate importance. Allah has placed the responsibility of faith on every individual's own shoulders; in this matter, blind following or forcing others to follow is not permissible. In the sight of God, no one will carry the burden of another. 'No bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (La taziru waziratuw-wizra ukhra). Here, this reality is clarified: those who reassure their dependents that they will carry their burdens are completely wrong and are speaking lies. On the Day of Judgment, they will carry their own burdens, and will also bear a portion of the burdens of those whom they led astray.

'About what they used to invent' ( 'amma kanoo yaftaroon) refers to those innovations they fabricated themselves but attributed to Allah, and forced others to follow, claiming they would bear their consequences. They will surely be questioned about all these fabrications on the Day of Judgment.

The upcoming subject... Verses 14-40
Three key points were mentioned in the previous verses:

First: Those who stand up for the cause of Allah do not reach their destination easily without bearing any hardship; rather, they must pass through various tests to attain the rank of the sincere and truthful.

Second: In the matter of religion, everyone is individually accountable before Allah, and no one else can bear their burden. Therefore, relying on the excuse of moral or physical pressure from others is not permissible.

Third: Those who inflict suffering on the followers of truth to turn them away from His path are granted respite to a certain limit, but if their rebellion increases, they are inevitably seized by His law, and once seized, they find no escape.

The upcoming verses present these realities in the light of the accounts of the prophets and their opponents. Read the verses in this light.

And We certainly sent Noah to his people, and he remained among them a thousand years minus fifty years, and the flood took them while they were wrongdoers. (14)

The mention of Noah's (peace be upon him) age of nine hundred and fifty years occurs only in this Surah. This precise duration is mentioned to fulfill the central theme of this Surah. It was explained earlier that those who choose the path of truth should not expect to reach their destination quickly without bearing any hardship; rather, they must face trials over a long period. To substantiate this reality, Noah's age is referenced, showing that he had to struggle alongside his people for a long period before succeeding in his test and completing his duty.

This duration is fully consistent with the Torah's narrative:

'After the flood Noah lived 350 years. Noah lived a total of 950 years, and then he died.' (Genesis 9:28-29)

The Torah records that during that early era, the average lifespan of humans was much longer. Noah's father lived for seven hundred and seventy-three years, and his grandfather lived for nine hundred and sixty-nine years. This duration is consistent with the average lifespan of that era. Although there is no documented history of that period besides the Torah, there is no reason to doubt its narrative. In that early age when the earth was populated and the modern corruptions had not yet developed, a longer average lifespan is consistent with divine wisdom and human nature.

'And the flood took them while they were wrongdoers' (fa-akhadhahumut-toofanu wa hum zalimoon). This supports the second reality mentioned in the introduction: although Allah grants respite to the opponents of truth, this respite has a limit, after which He inevitably seizes them. This seizure is not an injustice, but absolute justice, as proof had been fully established against them. According to the divine law, just as the trial of the followers of truth is essential before success, establishing proof against the followers of falsehood is necessary before punishment.

But We saved him and the companions of the ship, and We made it a sign for the worlds. (15)

Meaning, after this long struggle and trial, the stage arrived when Allah saved Noah (peace be upon him) and his companions and made this event a sign for the worlds, in which both the followers of truth and the followers of falsehood can see their respective ends.

The pronoun in 'And We made it' (Wa ja'alnaha) refers to the entire event narrated above, which is a known style in Arabic.

This story also contains the proof of the reality mentioned above: that in the matter of religion, no one can answer for another. If that were possible, Noah (peace be upon him) would have saved his son from His grasp, but since even a great prophet like Noah could not benefit his son, what can be said of others!

And [We sent] Abraham, when he said to his people, "Worship Allah and fear Him. That is best for you, if you should know." (16)

Meaning, just as We sent Noah (peace be upon him) as a messenger, We sent Abraham (peace be upon him) to his people. He also called his people to worship and fear Allah alone. To fear Him means not to associate partners with Him, which invites His wrath. 'That is best for you, if you should know' is a warning, implying: 'This is the best advice I am delivering to you, if you would only understand and value it.'

You only worship besides Allah idols, and you produce a falsehood. Indeed, those you worship besides Allah do not possess for you [the power of] provision. So seek from Allah provision and worship Him and be grateful to Him. To Him you will be returned. (17)

Meaning, the deities you worship besides Allah have no reality; they are mere stone idols of your own imagination. 'And you produce a falsehood' (wa takhluqoona ifka) means your claim that Allah has associated them with His divinity is a mere fabrication. Neither has Allah stated this, nor do you have any proof for it.

'Indeed, those you worship besides Allah do not possess for you [the power of] provision. So seek from Allah provision' means if you assume these idols have any control over your sustenance, it is a mere illusion. Every resource is in His power; therefore, if you seek sustenance, turn to Him and ask Him alone.

'And worship Him and be grateful to Him' means the only One who deserves your worship and gratitude is He who provides for you, so do not show ingratitude by associating partners with Him.

'To Him you will be returned' means you will be resurrected after death and will stand before Him alone; on that Day, these idols will not benefit you.

And if you deny - then already communities before you have denied. And there is not upon the messenger except clear notification. (18)

This is a severe warning: if you reject my words and persist in your denial, remember that many nations before you rejected their messengers, so be prepared to face the same end.

'And there is not upon the messenger except clear notification' means the messenger's responsibility is only to convey the message clearly. I have fulfilled this duty, and now the responsibility is yours; you will be called to account, not me.

Have they not seen how Allah originates creation and then repeats it? Indeed, that for Allah is easy. (19)

This and the subsequent verses up to verse 23 are a parenthetical addition by Allah within the account of Abraham's call. When Abraham's words reached the mention of the Resurrection, Allah added these arguments to complete his statement.

'Have they not seen how Allah originates creation and then repeats it?' (A-wa-lam yaraw kayfa yubdi'ullahul-khalaqa thumma yu'eeduh). If they have doubts about the Resurrection, have they not observed how Allah originates creation, causes it to perish, and then repeats it? This points to the argument presented in various styles earlier: when He has no difficulty in reviving a dead, barren land, why would He find it difficult to resurrect humans after they have turned to dust?

'Indeed, that for Allah is easy' (inna dhalika 'alallahi yaseer) means do not treat it as highly improbable; for the One who originated everything from nothing, repeating it is simple.

Say, "Travel through the land and observe how He originated creation. Then Allah will produce the final creation. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent." (20)

This encourages reflecting on the phenomena of this world with the correct perspective. Allah runs the system of this world as a complete school of learning. The arguments for monotheism, the Resurrection, and recompense are present within the daily phenomena of this world. Travel through the land and observe how He originates creation, causes one nation to perish and replaces it with another, and brings spring after autumn. These observations prove that nothing is difficult for Him, and He is competent over all things.

He punishes whom He wills and has mercy on whom He wills, and to Him you will be returned. (21)

This shows that the history of nations proves that He alone punishes whom He wills and shows mercy to whom He wills; none can influence His will except His own law of justice and wisdom. The history of nations related in the Qur'an is proof of this. It also shows that in the hereafter, the decision of reward and punishment will be entirely His; He will punish or forgive in accordance with His justice and wisdom.

'And to Him you will be returned' (wa ilayhi tuqlaboon) means everyone's return is to Him alone, and none other can become a refuge. Since none could save them from His grasp in this world, on what basis do they expect others to benefit them in the hereafter!

And you cannot escape Him on earth or in the heaven, and you have not besides Allah any protector or any helper. (22)

The previous verses utilized the third person, and now the text addresses the Quraysh directly to make the warning complete: 'If He wills to seize you, you cannot escape Him on earth or in the heaven, and you have no helper to protect you in this world or the hereafter.'

And those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah and His meeting - those have despaired of My mercy, and those will have a painful punishment. (23)

'Despaired' (ya'isoo) here carries the meaning of being deprived and distanced. It means they are permanently deprived of His mercy. Deprivation can sometimes carry the hope of recovery, which is less painful, but the deprivation of His mercy in the hereafter will be absolute and final.

It states that those who reject His signs and His meeting have despaired of His mercy, for the opportunity to deserve His mercy exists only in this life; those who waste this opportunity have no way to recover it.

And the answer of his people was not except that they said, "Kill him or burn him." But Allah saved him from the fire. Indeed, in that are signs for a people who believe. (24)

The parenthetical addition concludes, and the main narrative resumes. The response of Abraham's (peace be upon him) people was: 'Kill him or burn him.' They decided on the latter, and made a secret plan, but Allah saved him from their conspiracy. The text states: 'Indeed, in that are signs for a people who believe.'

First: Those who choose the path of truth must remember that they will face severe trials, and they cannot fulfill its demands without passing through them. Even a great prophet like Abraham had to pass through the fire.

Second: Filial bonds hold no value against the truth. Abraham's father pressured him to abandon the truth, but Abraham knew that his father would not answer for him on the Day of Judgment, so he stood firm against him despite his deep concern for him.

Third: Those who use their power to suppress the truth may receive respite for a time, in accordance with the law of trials, but if the followers of truth stand firm, the ultimate victory belongs to them, while the wrongdoers are humiliated.

And [Abraham] said, "You have only taken, other than Allah, idols as [a bond of] affection among you in worldly life. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will deny one another and curse one another, and your refuge will be the Fire, and you will not have any helpers." (25)

'Only' (Innama) denotes restriction, and 'affection among you' (mawaddatan baynakum) describes their state. The meaning is: 'These idols you worship besides Allah have no reality; they are mere figments of your imagination, and your devotion to them is limited to this worldly life. On the Day of Judgment, when the reality is exposed, you will deny and curse one another.' The followers will blame their leaders, and the leaders will declare their dissociation, as detailed throughout the Qur'an. This dispute is mentioned to warn those who claim they will bear the burdens of their dependents, showing that all worldly loyalties will vanish once the veil is lifted, and they will all end up in Hell without any helper.

And Lot believed in him. [Abraham] said, "Indeed, I will emigrate to my Lord. Indeed, He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (26)

'Believed in him' (Aamana lahu) carries the meaning of confirmation and support. Prophet Lot (peace be upon him) was Abraham's nephew and a prophet commissioned to his own people. The reference to his support highlights that at one point, Abraham had no supporter other than Lot, yet a time came when his call echoed throughout the world.

'And [Abraham] said: "Indeed, I will emigrate to my Lord. Indeed, He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise"' means when Abraham saw his people's hostility, he decided to emigrate, trusting that his Lord is Mighty and Wise—dominant over all things and wise in His plans, who would open a way for him through the darkness.

And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and placed in his descendants prophethood and scripture. And We gave him his reward in this world, and indeed, he is in the Hereafter among the righteous. (27)

This describes the favor bestowed upon Abraham (peace be upon him) after his emigration. Although he set out with no supporter other than Lot, Allah did not leave him alone, but blessed him with Isaac and Jacob, establishing a grand family of prophets and scriptures. He received his reward in this world, and will be among the righteous in the hereafter.

This contains implicit glad tidings for the Prophet and the Muslims of Makkah, who were facing intense trials. It consoles them: 'Do not fear migration; set out with trust in your Lord, and He will bless you with a grand future, just as He blessed Abraham.'

And [We sent] Lot, when he said to his people, "Indeed, you commit an obscenity such as no one of the worlds has preceded you in it." (28)

Lot (peace be upon him) was Abraham's nephew, commissioned to his own people. Since homosexual behavior had become a socially accepted fashion among them, he had to focus his efforts on its correction. He called it an 'obscenity' (fahishah) and warned them that they were the first nation to adopt this practice on a societal level.

'No one of the worlds has preceded you in it' (ma sabaqakum biha min ahadin minal-alameen) does not mean that no individual had ever committed this act before, but that no nation had ever adopted it as a socially accepted practice, which is why they deserved total destruction.

"Indeed, do you approach men and obstruct the road and commit in your meetings [every] evil?" And the answer of his people was not except that they said, "Bring us the punishment of Allah, if you should be of the truthful." (29)

'Obstruct the road' (taqta'oonas-sabeel) refers to deviating from the natural order established by the Creator. Lot warned them that this practice would destroy their family structure and lead to their ruin.

'And commit in your meetings [every] evil' (wa ta'toona fee nadeekumul-munkar) indicates that this was not a hidden sin, but was openly practiced and celebrated in their gatherings. This open celebration led to their ultimate moral degradation.

'And the answer of his people was not...' means they responded with defiance, challenging him to bring down the promised punishment if he was indeed a truthful messenger.

He said, "My Lord, support me against the corrupting people." (30)

When he saw no hope of their reform, Lot prayed to his Lord: 'My Lord, support me against this corrupt nation.'

And when Our messengers came to Abraham with the glad tidings, they said, "Indeed, we will destroy the people of this city. Indeed, its people have been wrongdoers." (31)

'Glad tidings' (bushra) refers to the glad tidings of a son and grandson given to Abraham (verse 27). The text states that the same angels who brought glad tidings to Abraham brought the decree of destruction to Lot's people. This highlights that mercy and punishment are in the same hand of the Creator. It also refutes the polytheists' belief in separate deities of good and evil.

'Indeed, its people have been wrongdoers' (inna ahlaha kanoo zalimeen) explains the cause of their destruction: they brought it upon themselves by their unnatural conduct and their defiant demand for punishment.

[Abraham] said, "Indeed, in it is Lot." They said, "We are more knowing of who is in it. We will surely save him and his family, except his wife; she is to be of those who remain behind." (32)

Upon learning of their mission, Abraham was concerned for Lot and his companions, asking: 'Indeed, in it is Lot.' The angels reassured him: 'We are fully aware of everyone in it. Rest assured; We will save Lot and his family, except his wife, who will remain behind to face the punishment.'

And when Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed for them and felt for them great uneasiness. And they said, "Do not fear and do not grieve. Indeed, we will save you and your family, except your wife; she is to be of those who remain behind." (33)

There is a linguistic feature here: when 'lamma' is followed by 'an', it denotes a close connection of cause and effect.

When the angels arrived at Lot's house, he felt distressed and uneasy. Why was he distressed? First, because they arrived in the form of beautiful young men, which was a final test for his people to expose their corruption. Since Lot did not know they were angels, he feared that the wicked chiefs of the town would try to violate them and humiliate him. His fear proved correct, as they surrounded his house. He pleaded with them, but they ignored him. Finally, the angels revealed their identity, assuring him: 'Do not fear; we are angels sent with the decree of destruction.' They also reassured him regarding his family, stating they would save him and his companions, except his wife.

His wife's end is mentioned twice to emphasize the reality that everyone is individually accountable; her relation to a prophet could not save her from the consequence of her own deeds.

"Indeed, we will bring down upon the people of this city a punishment from the sky because they have been defiantly disobedient." (34)

After reassuring Lot, the angels informed him of the decree: 'We will bring down a punishment from the sky because of their persistent disobedience.' 'Punishment' (rijz) refers to a terrifying calamity, and 'from the sky' denotes its absolute and inescapable nature.

And We have certainly left of it a sign as clear evidence for a people who use reason. (35)

Since their ruined dwellings lay along the trade routes of the Quraysh, they served as a clear sign of His law. But such signs benefit only those who use their reason, not those who treat history as mere legends.

And to Madyan [We sent] their brother Shu'ayb, and he said, "O my people, worship Allah and expect the Last Day and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption." But they denied him, so the earthquake seized them, and they became within their home bowed down. (36-37)

While the previous accounts were detailed, the subsequent ones are mentioned briefly to establish the core lesson: 'Or do those who reflect evil deeds think they will outrun Us? Evil is what they judge' (verse 4). The upcoming historical accounts prove that the respite granted to the wrongdoers does not mean they can escape His grasp. The text references Madyan, 'Ad, Thamud, and individuals like Karun, Pharaoh, and Haman, warning the Quraysh that their predecessors possessed far more power, yet could not escape His grasp when they persisted in their rebellion.

The people of Madyan were successful merchants, much like the Quraysh, which is why their story is referenced first. Shu'ayb called them to worship Allah, fear the Last Day, and avoid corruption, but they rejected him, and were destroyed by a violent earthquake within their own homes, as detailed in Surah Hud.

And [We destroyed] 'Ad and Thamud, and it has become clear to you from their dwellings. And Satan made their deeds fair-seeming to them and averted them from the path, though they were endowed with perception. (38)

A verb is omitted before 'Ad, meaning: 'And We destroyed 'Ad and Thamud.' The ruins of their dwellings lay along the trade routes of the Quraysh, who are urged to reflect on their end. 'Though they were endowed with perception' (wa kanoo mustabsireen) means they were highly capable in worldly affairs, sciences, and politics, yet this worldly cleverness could not save them from the traps of Satan. He made their worldly pursuits so fair-seeming to them that they became blind to the afterlife, and fell into destruction. This shows that worldly progress is no proof of being on the straight path; without spiritual vision, worldly cleverness only leads to ultimate ruin.

And [We destroyed] Karun and Pharaoh and Haman. And Moses had already come to them with clear evidences, but they were arrogant in the land, and they were not outrunners. (39)

These names are also connected to the omitted verb. After mentioning the nations, three prominent individuals are mentioned: Karun, Pharaoh, and Haman. As noted, Karun's counterpart among the Quraysh was Abu Lahab, so this is a warning to their leaders. Moses showed them clear signs, but they remained arrogant in the land. 'And they were not outrunners' (wa ma kanoo sabiqeen) means they could not escape His grasp despite their wealth, power, and armies.

So each We seized for his sin; and among them were those upon whom We sent a storm of stones, and among them were those who were seized by the shriek, and among them were those whom We caused the earth to swallow, and among them were those whom We drowned. And Allah would not have wronged them, but it was they who were wrongdoing themselves. (40)

This summarizes the end of these nations and individuals: each was seized for his own sin. Lot's people faced a storm of stones; 'Ad, Thamud, and Madyan faced a shriek; Karun was swallowed by the earth; and Pharaoh and Haman were drowned.

'And Allah would not have wronged them, but it was they who were wrongdoing themselves' means they brought this end upon themselves by rejecting the warnings of the messengers.

The upcoming subject... Verses 41-69
Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, summarizing the previous lessons:

First: The house built on polytheism is like a spider's web—completely fragile and baseless, offering no security in this world or the hereafter.

Second: The believers should rest assured that Allah has created this world with truth and purpose, and His justice will surely prevail; therefore, remain steadfast, invite to the truth, and establish prayer.

Third: Guidelines for dealing with the People of the Book—do not engage with their wrongdoers, but invite the sincere among them using the common ground between you. This includes arguments for the Prophet's messengership.

Fourth: Encouraging the weak Muslims to migrate, assuring them of His provision and care.

Fifth: A severe warning to the Quraysh: their opposition to the Qur'an is contrary to their own admissions, and they are highly ungrateful. They will soon face the consequences, while those who strive for His cause will be guided to His paths. Read the verses in this light.

The parable of those who take besides Allah protectors is like the parable of the spider which takes a home. And indeed, the weakest of homes is the home of the spider, if they only knew. (41)

This historical evidence proves that none can save a person from Allah's grasp. The idols and protectors associated with Him are completely helpless. The home they have built on polytheism, thinking it to be secure, is like a spider's web—completely fragile and unable to withstand even a gentle breeze. 'If they only knew' (law kanoo ya'lamoon) means this reality has been made clear, but they lack the capacity to understand it.

Indeed, Allah knows whatever thing they invoke besides Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise. (42)

This is not a mere hyperbole, but a statement of fact: Allah is fully aware of the helplessness of these false deities. 'And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise' (Wa Huwal-Azizul-Hakeem) means these idols cannot stand against the One who is All-Mighty and All-Wise. Since He is dominant, none can stay His hand, and since He is Wise, His law of justice cannot be averted. The polytheists believed their idols would save them from His grasp, but the text refutes this false belief through His attributes.

And these parables We present to the people, but none will understand them except those of knowledge. (43)

'These parables' refers to all the parables and historical accounts related above. They are presented not for entertainment, but for people to reflect on them and learn lessons. However, learning lessons from others' history is the quality of those who use their reason. 'Those of knowledge' ('alimoon) refers to those who keep their natural faculties alive and use their reason to learn from every event. In the Qur'an's terminology, a scholar is not one who merely carries a load of books, but one who reflects on His signs and learns from them.

Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth. Indeed, in that is a sign for the believers. (44)

Anyone who reflects on the heavens and the earth will realize that this universe is not a purposeless creation, but was created by an All-Wise, All-Powerful Creator with a purposeful truth (bil-haqq), which will be fully manifest on the Day of Judgment when His justice is established.

'Indeed, in that is a sign for the believers' (Inna fee dhalika la-aayatan lil-mu'mineen) contains several key lessons:

First: It points to the logical necessity of the Day of Judgment. Since the Creator is Wise, He must establish a Day of complete justice, otherwise creation would be a purposeless play.

Second: The only successful life is one lived with focus on the hereafter, for the ultimate decision rests there. This requires evaluating every worldly gain or loss in the light of its consequences in the hereafter.

Third: Polytheism is completely false, as it contradicts the purposeful nature of the universe.

Further explanation of this will follow under Surah ar-Room, verse 8.

Recite what has been revealed to you of the Book and establish prayer. Indeed, prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing, and the remembrance of Allah is greater. And Allah knows what you do. (45)

This consoles the Prophet and the Muslims: recite and convey this Qur'an to the people. If they ignore its arguments and demand miracles, do not worry about them. Your duty is only to convey the message.

'And establish prayer. Indeed, prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing' (wa aqimis-salat, innas-salata tanha 'anul-fahsha'i wal-munkar). This emphasizes the night prayer (Tahajjud) to foster patience and steadfastness among the Muslims during this challenging phase.

Two duties are enjoined here: reciting the revelation (concerning the call to others) and establishing prayer (concerning the spiritual training of the believers). The Prophet is told to focus on conveying the message, while arranging for the spiritual training of the believers through prayer, which will purify them from the moral evils that destroyed past nations.

'Immorality and wrongdoing' (fahsha' and munkar) encompass all moral evils. Immorality (fahsha') refers to those evils arising from the deviation of primal desires, which can infect an entire society if left unchecked, as in the case of Lot's people. Wrongdoing (munkar) refers to those evils arising from greed and love of wealth and power, which oppose the established values of a righteous society, as in the case of 'Ad, Thamud, and Madyan.

Prayer acts as a powerful adviser against these evils, reminding the believer of His rights and duties. However, this potential is realized only when prayer is observed with sincerity, presence of heart, and understanding.

'And the remembrance of Allah is greater' (wa ladhikrullahi akbar) means the remembrance of Allah through prayer is the greatest source of spiritual strength and peace of mind (al-Ra'd: 28), which enables the believer to stand firm against all trials. This spiritual strength is distinct from mere worldly courage.

'And Allah knows what you do' (Wallahu ya'lamu ma tasna'oon) reassures the believers that their struggles in His cause are in His knowledge, and He will reward them fully.

And do not argue with the People of the Book except in a way that is best, except for those who commit injustice among them, and say, "We believe in that which has been revealed to us and revealed to you. And our God and your God is one, and we are Muslims in submission to Him." (46)

During this phase, the People of the Book had also joined the opposition, which is why these guidelines are provided for dealing with them. Since their status was different from the Arab polytheists, the method of addressing them had to be refined.

It is stated: 'Do not argue with the People of the Book except in a way that is best.' 'Argue' (la tujadiloo) here is used in the sense of discussion and presenting arguments. The best way is to focus on the common values between you, which does not offend their pride and encourages the sincere among them to reflect on the truth.

'Except for those who commit injustice among them' (Illa-lladheena zalamoo minhum) means those who are insincere and highly hostile should not be engaged with at all, as is established throughout the Qur'an.

'And say: "We believe in that which has been revealed to us and revealed to you. And our God and your God is one, and we are Muslims in submission to Him"' is the explanation of the best way: tell them that we believe in the Qur'an and in the previous scriptures, as the Qur'an confirms them rather than denying them. The predictions of your scriptures find their fulfillment in this final revelation; therefore, you should be the first to welcome it. We both believe in one God, and our call is to submit to Him alone.

And thus We have sent down to you the Book. And those to whom We gave the Scripture believe in it. And of these [people of Makkah] are some who believe in it. And none reject Our verses except the disbelievers. (47)

'And thus We have sent down to you the Book' means We have revealed this Book on the same principle explained above—confirming the true essence of all previous revelations and completing the guidance.

'And those to whom We gave the Scripture believe in it' (falladheena aataynahumul-Kitaba yu'minoona bihi). As explained elsewhere, the passive form refers to the righteous among them, who will accept this Book because they recognize its truth.

'And of these are some who believe in it' means some among the people of Makkah are accepting it, and others will follow as long as they retain sincerity.

'And none reject Our verses except the disbelievers' (wa ma yajhadu bi-aayatina illal-kafiroon) means none reject His verses except those who have completely closed their minds to any truth.

And you did not recite before it any scripture, nor did you write one with your right hand; otherwise the falsifiers would have doubted. (48)

This points to a powerful proof of the Prophet's messengership: how could an unlettered person, who had never read any scripture nor knew how to write, present such profound wisdom? This is a clear proof that this message is a revelation from Allah.

'Otherwise the falsifiers would have doubted' (idhal-lartabal-mubtiloon) means had you been lettered, they might have claimed you compiled this message from previous sources, but under the circumstances, their doubts are completely baseless.

Rather, it is clear verses in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge. And none reject Our verses except the wrongdoers. (49)

For those who possess true knowledge among the People of the Book, the Qur'an is an obvious, familiar truth that resonates with their hearts, in accordance with the predictions of their scriptures.

'And none reject Our verses except the wrongdoers' (wa ma yajhadu bi-aayatina illaz-zalimoon) is a restatement of the theme of verse 47: none reject His verses except those who have darkened their own hearts through persistent injustice. 'Wrongdoers' (zalimoon) refers to those hostile opponents mentioned in verse 46.

And they say, "Why are not signs sent down to him from his Lord?" Say, "The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a clear warner." (50)

This quotes the objection of the People of the Book, who tried to instigate the Quraysh by asking why the Prophet was not given physical miracles like those of Moses and Jesus. The response is addressed to the Prophet, showing complete indifference toward them.

'Say: "The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a clear warner"' means: 'Signs and miracles are in Allah's hands alone, and He displays them in accordance with His wisdom. My duty is only to warn you of the consequences of rejection; I have not claimed divinity to display miracles on your demand.'

And is it not sufficient for them that We revealed to you the Book which is recited to them? Indeed, in that is a mercy and reminder for a people who believe. (51)

This is a comprehensive response: is the revelation of this Book, which is recited to them and is its own proof, not a sufficient sign of your messengership? When this grand sign is present, what is the need for other signs?

'Indeed, in that is a mercy and reminder for a people who believe' (Inna fee dhalika la-rahmatan-wa dhikra li-qawmin yu'minoon) means the revelation of this Book is a great favor and mercy. Physical miracles are temporary, but this Book is a lasting mercy and reminder for those who value the truth. 'Reminder' (dhikra) highlights that the Qur'an reminds humans of the truths inherent in their own nature, which they only need to reflect upon to recognize.

Say, "Sufficient is Allah between me and you as a witness. He knows what is in the heavens and the earth." And those who believe in falsehood and disbelieve in Allah - those are the losers. (52)

Meaning, their objections are mere excuses; they internally recognize your truthfulness but choose to reject it. Therefore, leave their matter to Allah, saying: 'Sufficient is Allah between me and you as a witness. He is fully aware of all secrets and will judge between us on the Day of Judgment. Those who choose to believe in falsehood and reject Allah are indeed the losers.'

And they urge you to hasten the punishment. And if not for a decreed term, the punishment would have come upon them. And it will surely come upon them suddenly while they perceive not. (53)

When the Prophet warned them of punishment, they would mockingly demand its immediate descent. The text states: 'Allah has appointed a term for the destruction of nations, and the punishment does not come before that term. Had He not established this law, the punishment would have struck them immediately.' The duration of respite is determined by His wisdom, and when that term arrives, the punishment will strike them suddenly, without any warning.

They urge you to hasten the punishment. And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers on the Day the punishment covers them from above them and from below their feet and it is said, "Taste what you used to do." (54-55)

The demand for punishment is repeated to express surprise at their unmindfulness: they are mockingly demanding punishment as if it were far off, whereas they are already encompassed by it. Their sinful deeds are the very source of their punishment, which will envelop them completely on the Day of Judgment, when they will be told: 'Taste the consequence of what you used to do.'

O My servants who have believed, indeed My earth is spacious, so worship Me alone. (56)

Addressing the weak Muslims who were facing trials in Makkah, Allah comforts them in an affectionate manner: 'O My servants who have believed, if you find it difficult to practice your faith in Makkah, My earth is spacious; emigrate to another land where you can worship Me freely. Stand firm on My worship, and do not accept the humiliation of worshipping others. If you leave your homes for My sake, your provision is My responsibility, and My treasures are boundless.'

This verse highlights that:

First: Emigration (hijrah) is necessary when practicing one's faith becomes impossible.

Second: A believer must remain firm on His worship under all circumstances.

Third: One should not hesitate to leave everything for His sake, as Allah is the Provider.

Every soul will taste death. Then to Us you will be returned. (57)

This is a source of comfort and strength: this life is temporary, and everyone must ultimately return to Us. Therefore, why should a person compromise his faith for this short-lived existence?

And those who believe and do righteous deeds - We will surely dwell them in chambers of Paradise beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally. Excellent is the reward of the [righteous] workers. (58)

Meaning, those who live a life of faith and righteous deeds will be blessed with high chambers in Paradise, where they will abide eternally. This eternal reward is far greater than any temporary worldly comfort, and is what a wise person should strive for.

Who have been patient and upon their Lord rely. (59)

This describes the qualities of the righteous workers, showing that those who are patient and place their trust in their Lord are the ones who can stand firm against all trials and make sacrifices.

And how many a creature carries not its provision. Allah provides for it and for you. And He is the Hearing, the Knowing. (60)

This reassures those who hesitated to emigrate out of concern for their livelihood: 'Do not worry about your provision. Look at the countless creatures on earth who do not carry their own provision, yet Allah provides for them. The same Lord who provides for them will provide for you. He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing, fully aware of your needs and prayers.'

Lord Jesus expressed this same wisdom as follows:

'Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat or drink; or about your body, what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothes? Look at the birds of the air; they do not sow or reap or store away in barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not much more valuable than they? Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life? And why do you worry about clothes? See how the flowers of the field grow. They do not labor or spin. Yet I tell you that not even Solomon in all his splendor was dressed like one of these. If that is how God clothes the grass of the field, which is here today and tomorrow is thrown into the fire, will he not much more clothe you—you of little faith? So do not worry, saying, "What shall we eat?" or "What shall we drink?" or "What shall we wear?" For the pagans run after all these things, and your heavenly Father knows that you need them. But seek first his kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well. Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own.' (Matthew 6:25-34)

The Qur'an has beautifully summarized this extensive wisdom in a single verse.

And if you ask them, "Who created the heavens and the earth and subjected the sun and the moon?" they will surely say, "Allah." Then how are they deluded? (61)

This presents the arguments for monotheism and trust in Him: if you ask the polytheists who created the heavens and the earth and subjected the sun and the moon, they will acknowledge that it is Allah. After admitting this, how can they turn away and worship others? This highlights their absolute inconsistency: they acknowledged Him as the sole Creator, yet associated partners with Him in His authority.

Allah extends provision for whom He wills of His servants and restricts it for him. Indeed, Allah is of all things Knowing. (62)

Since He is the Creator and Owner of all things, He alone extends or restricts provision in accordance with His wisdom and knowledge. None can influence His decision, as He knows best the needs of His creation.

And if you ask them, "Who sends down from the sky water by which He gives life to the earth after its death?" they will surely say, "Allah." Say, "Praise be to Allah!" But most of them do not use reason. (63)

Similarly, if you ask them who sends down rain from the sky to revive the dead land, they will admit that it is Allah. Say: 'Praise be to Allah!' Since He is the sole Provider of these blessings, He alone deserves your gratitude and worship.

'But most of them do not use reason' (bal aktharuhum la ya'qiloon) points to their inconsistency: they admitted Him as the Provider, yet directed their gratitude to others.

And this worldly life is not except amusement and diversion. And indeed, the home of the Hereafter - that is the [eternal] life, if they only knew. (64)

This points to the cause of their deviation: their obsession with the temporary pleasures of this life. This worldly life is but temporary amusement; the true, eternal life is in the hereafter. Worldly life holds value only when lived as a preparation for the hereafter; otherwise, it is a mere play that leads to ultimate loss.

And when they board a ship, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion. But when He delivers them to the land, at once they associate partners with Him, so they may be ungrateful for what We have given them, and that they may enjoy themselves. But they are going to know. (65-66)

Their unmindfulness is compared to the passengers of a ship: when the wind is favorable, they enjoy themselves, but the moment a storm strikes, they call upon Allah alone, promising sincere devotion. Yet, once they are safely delivered to the land, they return to their old ways of polytheism. This ungrateful conduct only leads to their ultimate ruin.

This is identical to: 'And when harm touches people, they call upon their Lord, turning to Him; then when He lets them taste mercy from Him, at once a party of them associate partners with their Lord, so they may be ungrateful...' (ar-Room: 33-34).

Have they not seen that We made [Makkah] a safe sanctuary, while people are swept away all around them? Then in falsehood do they believe, and in the favor of Allah do they disbelieve? (67)

This applies the parable of the ship to the Quraysh: they enjoy peace and security in the safe sanctuary of Makkah, while people all around them are in constant danger, yet they associate partners with Him within His sanctuary. They believe in falsehood and reject His favor.

This has been discussed under Surah al-Qasas, verse 57.

And who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah or denies the truth when it has come to him? Is there not in Hell a residence for the disbelievers? (68)

This is the final indictment: who is more unjust than one who associates partners with Allah or rejects His Messenger and Book when they have arrived? 'Is there not in Hell a residence for the disbelievers?' means their dwelling in Hell is absolute.

And those who strive for Us - We will surely guide them to Our ways. And indeed, Allah is with the doers of good. (69)

Turning back to the weak Muslims, Allah promises them: 'We will surely guide them to Our ways.' This promise is for guidance in religion, ease in the world, and salvation in the hereafter.

'And indeed, Allah is with the doers of good' (Wa innallaha la-ma'al-muhsineen) is a grand promise of His support for those who face trials with patience, trust, and beautiful conduct.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed by the grace of Allah. Praise be to Allah for this.
